topic: Type System - Trait Objects, dyn Trait, and vtables

turns.content: I'm trying to build a plugin system in my Rust side project where different game modules need to share an interface. I keep seeing people use &dyn Trait everywhere but sometimes Box<dyn Trait>. What's the actual difference between these two ways of wrapping a trait object?

The core distinction is that a reference like &dyn MyTrait points to existing heap data, whereas Box<dyn MyTrait> allocates new memory. When you use &dyn, the pointer arithmetic stays within your current allocation boundaries, while Box creates a fresh fat pointer with its own size and alignment rules. This matters because if you try to store &dyn inside a struct that needs to be Send or Sync, you might run into ownership issues unless you carefully manage the lifetime.
So when I write Box<dyn Trait> it allocates on the heap right? Does that mean every time I create a plugin I'm doing an extra allocation compared to just passing a reference?

Yes, exactly. Box<dyn Trait> performs one heap allocation for the trait object itself plus whatever data the concrete type holds inside it. If you're passing &dyn by value across function boundaries without taking ownership, you avoid that extra allocation but lose move semantics. The vtable pointer lives at the start of your fat pointer structure regardless of whether you use a reference or Box, so both approaches have similar indirect call overhead when you actually invoke methods.

That sounds inefficient for performance-critical game code. Is there any way to avoid the vtable lookup entirely? I've heard about something called a monomorphized interface or maybe using generics instead?

You could theoretically use generic trait objects with associated types to reduce overhead, but Rust's type system doesn't support true polymorphic dispatch without vtables. The only real way to eliminate the vtable is to use explicit generics where every concrete implementation knows its exact memory layout at compile time. For most plugin systems though, dyn Trait is acceptable because modern CPUs can handle indirect calls efficiently enough for frame rates.
Can you show me how the vtable actually works in memory? Like what exactly does a fat pointer contain when I have Box<dyn GameModule>?

A fat pointer for Box<dyn GameModule> contains two things: a raw pointer to the actual data and an index into the vtable. The vtable itself is just a sequence of function pointers ordered by how they appear in your trait definition, not sorted or optimized. When you call method Foo on that boxed object, the compiler generates code that loads both values from memory and performs indirect dispatch through that fixed sequence.
What happens if I try to add Send + Sync bounds to my dyn Trait? Does that change anything about how the vtable is laid out or accessed?

Adding Send + Sync doesn't alter the memory layout of the fat pointer itself. Those traits only constrain what lifetimes and ownership patterns are allowed when you create or move values containing that trait object. The vtable sequence remains identical whether your trait is bounded by Send or not; it's purely about compile-time checks ensuring threads can safely share instances.

I'm confused about upcasting then. If I have a concrete implementation of MyPlugin and want to store it in a collection of &dyn GameModule, do I need to explicitly cast or does Rust handle that automatically?

Rust handles the conversion implicitly when you pass a value implementing the trait into a function expecting &dyn Trait. The compiler performs safe upcasting by checking that all methods in your concrete type match those in the trait object. You never manually write cast operations unless you're working with raw pointers or unsafe code blocks.
What if I need to store many different plugin types together? Can I just use a Vec<dyn GameModule> and iterate over it without knowing which specific type each element is?

Absolutely. A Vec<dyn GameModule> lets you hold heterogeneous implementations uniformly because the vtable provides the dispatch mechanism at runtime. You can call common methods like activate() or deactivate() on every element without knowing their concrete types ahead of time, though you won't have access to type-specific fields unless you use trait methods.
One more thing - what if I want to optimize my plugin loading? Should I avoid dyn Trait altogether and just use concrete types directly?

If performance profiling shows significant overhead from indirect calls, then yes. For tight loops or critical game logic paths, replacing dyn Trait with concrete generics eliminates the vtable entirely. However, for a plugin system where you need extensibility and runtime discovery, dyn Trait usually provides an acceptable balance between flexibility and performance.
So my main takeaway is that Box vs &dyn depends on whether I need ownership transfer or just borrowing within the same allocation?

Precisely. Use &dyn when you're passing references around and want to avoid extra allocations, then use Box<dyn> only when you actually need to take ownership across function boundaries or store the trait object inside a struct that needs heap management.
